evals.prompt: Our PDP feels cluttered. What should we keep and what should we cut?
evals.expected_output: Walks the PDP-element-ordering framework. Should identify the seven structural elements and their canonical eye-path ordering: hero → price → CTA → variant → bullets → social proof → related. Should warn against adding too many secondary CTAs (quiz, bundle promo, subscribe callout, referral prompt). Should recommend picking one secondary CTA at most and subordinating the rest. Should ask what device the user is auditing for.
assertions: Checks for .agents/modern-ai-context.md
Walks the PDP-element-ordering framework
Names the eye-path ordering (hero, price, CTA, variant, bullets, social proof, related)
Warns against multiple competing secondary CTAs
Recommends one secondary CTA at most
Calls modern.sales.aov segmented by page_template
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact Changes, Test Ideas sections
Names a single highest-leverage next move

evals.prompt: Should we curate our collection pages or use algorithmic sorting?
evals.expected_output: Walks the curated-vs-algorithmic framework. Should not give a generic answer; should ask about catalog size and brand voice. Recommends curated for small to mid-size catalogs and editorial-voice brands, algorithmic for large catalogs with personalization signals. Should recommend the hybrid: curated guardrails on algorithmic collections (a human-curated first row, algorithm fills the rest). Should warn against pure algorithmic with no human guardrails.
assertions: Walks the curated-vs-algorithmic framework
Asks about catalog size and brand voice
Recommends curated for small catalogs and editorial-voice brands
Recommends algorithmic for large catalogs with personalization
Recommends the hybrid with curated first row over algorithmic remainder
Warns against pure algorithmic with no human guardrails
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact Changes, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Where should we put product recommendations? PDP, cart, post-purchase?
evals.expected_output: Walks the recommendation-surface-placement framework. Should rank surfaces by conversion per impression: cart drawer (5-12%) > cart cross-sell (3-8%) > post-purchase upsell (5-15%) > PDP related (1-4%) > homepage > email. Should recommend investing in cart-drawer first because it is often the highest-leverage underutilized surface. Should connect to SKU-affinity logic.
assertions: Walks the recommendation-surface-placement framework
Ranks surfaces by conversion per impression
Names cart drawer as highest conversion surface
Recommends investing in cart drawer first as underutilized
Connects to SKU-affinity logic
Calls modern.sales.sku_affinity via modern-mcp if connected
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact Changes, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: We label our newest product as 'best-seller' to drive attention. Is that bad?
evals.expected_output: Direct answer: yes, this is the badge-abuse anti-pattern. Should walk the best-seller-badging-discipline framework. Best-seller should be a real signal (top 5 SKUs by units or top 10 by revenue in trailing 30 days). Should explain that customers detect inconsistency between badged products and actual store traffic, and trust erodes. Should recommend rotating best-seller list, category-specific badging, and discipline on adjacent badges (customer favorite, new, editor's pick).
assertions: Identifies the practice as badge-abuse anti-pattern
Walks the best-seller-badging-discipline framework
Defines best-seller as a real signal (top SKUs by units or revenue)
Explains trust erosion when badges don't match actual traffic
Recommends rotating best-seller list
Discusses adjacent badge discipline (customer favorite, new, editor's pick)
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact Changes, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Our holiday merchandising flopped. Calendar said December but sales were flat. What went wrong?
evals.expected_output: Walks the seasonal-merchandising-rhythm framework. Should explain that seasonal merchandising requires alignment of three inputs: calendar, behavior, inventory. Calendar alone is insufficient. Should ask what behavior signals were monitored (search-term volume, returning-visitor frequency, cart abandonment patterns) and what inventory state was. Should diagnose likely failure modes: gift-friendly bundles vs regular catalog with snowflake banner; out-of-stock by mid-month; behavior shifts that calendar missed.
assertions: Walks the seasonal-merchandising-rhythm framework
Names the three inputs (calendar, behavior, inventory)
Explains that calendar alone is insufficient
Asks what behavior signals were monitored
Asks what inventory state was during the campaign
Names common failure modes (regular catalog with banner, mid-month stock-out, behavior shifts)
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact Changes, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: We're surfacing an out-of-stock SKU as our top recommendation. How big a deal is that?
evals.expected_output: Direct answer that this is the inventory-aware-merchandising failure mode. Should walk the framework: out-of-stock SKUs should not appear in first row of collections, cart-drawer recommendations, or post-purchase surfaces. Should recommend integrating inventory data into merchandising surfaces in real-time. Should also identify the opposite mistake: overstock SKUs languishing instead of being promoted. Should recommend a weekly merchandising review at minimum.
assertions: Identifies the inventory-aware-merchandising failure
Walks the inventory-aware-merchandising framework
Recommends removing out-of-stock from first row, cart drawer, post-purchase
Recommends integrating inventory data in real-time
Identifies the opposite mistake of overstock languishing
Recommends regular merchandising review cadence
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact Changes, Test Ideas sections

evals.prompt: Our collection grids look fine but conversion is flat. What should we look at?
evals.expected_output: Walks the visual-hierarchy-on-collection-grids framework. Should audit first row vs later rows, image consistency, pricing visibility (hidden price is a common conversion killer), variant indicators on grid cards, and loaded-rows pacing on long collections. Should note that brands often over-invest in PDP design and under-invest in collection grids, even though most product discovery happens on collection pages.
assertions: Walks the visual-hierarchy-on-collection-grids framework
Audits first row vs later rows
Names image consistency as a scan-speed lever
Names price visibility as a conversion lever (hidden price is bad)
Names variant indicators on grid cards
Names loaded-rows pacing for long collections
Notes the over-investment in PDP vs collection grids
Output has Quick Wins, High-Impact Changes, Test Ideas sections
